questions.question: Which dynasty was the last imperial dynasty to rule China?
questions.explanation: The Qing Dynasty ruled China from 1644 until the formation of the Republic of China in 1912.

presentation.questionStyled: Name the final **imperial dynasty** of China, assuming you've ever glanced at a map.
presentation.explanationStyled: The Qing Dynasty. It collapsed in 1912. Try to remember that for five minutes.
presentation.hostLeadIn: This ended thousands of years of imperial rule, which you'd know if you studied.
wrongAnswerQuips: Ming came before, but keep guessing.
Han was centuries earlier. Just stop.
Tang ended in 907. You're hopeless.

questions.question: In which modern-day country is the ancient archaeological site of Petra located?
questions.explanation: Petra is a historical and archaeological city in southern Jordan, famous for its rock-cut architecture.

presentation.questionStyled: Where in the world is the ancient site of **Petra**? I'm waiting.
presentation.explanationStyled: Jordan. It is a very famous desert city. Try to keep up with the syllabus.
presentation.hostLeadIn: This was the Nabataean capital. Surely even you have heard of it.
wrongAnswerQuips: Egypt has pyramids, not Petra. Genius.
Iraq has Babylon. Wrong country.
Syria is not the right answer. How disappointing.

questions.question: The 'Rose Revolution' of 2003 took place in which former Soviet republic?
questions.explanation: The Rose Revolution was a pro-Western change of power in Georgia that led to the resignation of Eduard Shevardnadze.

presentation.questionStyled: Identify the country where the **'Rose Revolution'** actually occurred.
presentation.explanationStyled: Georgia. It really isn't that difficult to locate on a map.
presentation.hostLeadIn: Color revolutions are not just fancy names; they are actual history.
wrongAnswerQuips: Ukraine was Orange. Wrong color.
Belarus? You wish. Incorrect.
Uzbekistan is not the answer. What a surprise.

questions.question: Which 14th-century ruler of the Mali Empire is often cited as the wealthiest individual in history?
questions.explanation: Mansa Musa is famous for his pilgrimage to Mecca, during which he distributed so much gold that he caused inflation in Cairo.

presentation.questionStyled: Who was the ruler of the **Mali Empire** so wealthy it's offensive? Go.
presentation.explanationStyled: Mansa Musa. His gold reserves were legendary.
presentation.hostLeadIn: Timbuktu was his city. Try to act like you've heard of it.
wrongAnswerQuips: Sundiata was the founder. Keep guessing.
Sonni Ali? No. Try again.
Askia was later. You're wasting my time.

questions.question: Which noble gas has the atomic number 10?
questions.explanation: Neon (Ne) has 10 protons and is located in group 18 of the periodic table.

presentation.questionStyled: Atomic number 10. Give me the name, and don't make me repeat the periodic table.
presentation.explanationStyled: It is Neon. It’s glowing in every city. How did you miss this?
presentation.hostLeadIn: The periodic table isn't that large. Try using it.
wrongAnswerQuips: Helium is 2. Do you even science?
Argon is 18. Not even close.
Krypton is 36. Just stop answering.

questions.question: What is the exact speed of light in a vacuum, in meters per second?
questions.explanation: The exact speed of light in a vacuum is 299,792,458 meters per second.

presentation.questionStyled: What is the **speed of light** in a vacuum? Give me the exact number.
presentation.explanationStyled: 299,792,458 m/s. Please try to remember it so I don't have to.
presentation.hostLeadIn: It's a constant. It doesn't care about your inability to memorize it.
wrongAnswerQuips: That is painfully slow. Laughable.
Not even close to the scale.
That's per hour. Keep trying.

questions.question: As of 2023, which planet in our solar system is recognized as having the most confirmed moons?
questions.explanation: Saturn currently holds the record with 146 confirmed moons, following new discoveries in 2023.

presentation.questionStyled: Which planet has the most **moons**? This has changed recently, keep up.
presentation.explanationStyled: It is Saturn. 146 confirmed. Jupiter isn't the king anymore.
presentation.hostLeadIn: Space is evolving, unlike your test scores.
wrongAnswerQuips: Jupiter lost the title. Embarrassing.
Neptune is empty by comparison.
Uranus doesn't even have 30. Sad.

questions.question: Which movie became the first non-English language film to win the Academy Award for Best Picture?
questions.explanation: The South Korean film Parasite, directed by Bong Joon-ho, won Best Picture at the 92nd Academy Awards in 2020.

presentation.questionStyled: Which **non-English film** actually won Best Picture? It wasn't that long ago.
presentation.explanationStyled: Parasite. It won in 2020. Try to keep up with modern cinema.
presentation.hostLeadIn: International cinema isn't obscure. It's mainstream.
wrongAnswerQuips: The Artist was silent. Nice guess.
Roma didn't win that category.
Crouching Tiger? You are outdated.

questions.question: Who was the first actor to play the character James Bond in a feature-length film?
questions.explanation: Sean Connery starred as James Bond in the first film, Dr. No, released in 1962.

presentation.questionStyled: Who was the **first James Bond**? If you guess Daniel Craig, leave.
presentation.explanationStyled: Sean Connery. He started it all in 'Dr. No'.
presentation.hostLeadIn: The name's Bond. If you get this wrong, I will sigh deeply.
wrongAnswerQuips: Moore came way later. Focus.
Lazenby did one film. Fail.
Niven? You're just pulling names out of thin air.

questions.question: Which French sculptor designed the Statue of Liberty?
questions.explanation: Frédéric Auguste Bartholdi designed the statue, while Gustave Eiffel designed the internal framework.

presentation.questionStyled: Who designed the **Statue of Liberty**? I expect a real answer.
presentation.explanationStyled: Bartholdi. It was a gift. Try to remember it.
presentation.hostLeadIn: A French gift. How historic.
wrongAnswerQuips: Rodin? He does men thinking. Wrong.
Degas? Dancers. Wrong again.
Duchamp? A urinal. Definitely not.

questions.question: Which Pablo Picasso painting depicts the bombing of a Basque town during the Spanish Civil War?
questions.explanation: Guernica is a large oil painting on canvas by Pablo Picasso completed in June 1937, following the bombing of Guernica.

presentation.questionStyled: Name the Picasso painting about a **Basque bombing**. It's iconic.
presentation.explanationStyled: Guernica. It's massive and incredibly powerful. Try viewing it sometime.
presentation.hostLeadIn: Art makes political statements. This one is brutal.
wrongAnswerQuips: That's Cubist, but wrong title.
Guitarist? Wrong era. Pathetic.
Weeping Woman? Wrong theme. Try again.

questions.question: As of 2024, which NBA team has won the most championships in league history?
questions.explanation: The Boston Celtics won their 18th NBA championship in 2024, breaking their tie with the Lakers.

presentation.questionStyled: Who has the **most NBA titles** as of 2024? Keep up with the headlines.
presentation.explanationStyled: The Boston Celtics. 18 rings. Get it right.
presentation.hostLeadIn: Championships define a franchise. This one defines success.
wrongAnswerQuips: Lakers are second. Try again.
Bulls? Stick to 90s history.
Warriors? You aren't watching basketball.

questions.question: Which programming language was created by Guido van Rossum and first released in 1991?
questions.explanation: Guido van Rossum designed Python as a successor to the ABC language.

presentation.questionStyled: Which language did **Guido van Rossum** make? Code something.
presentation.explanationStyled: Python. It's clean, simple, and you still managed to guess wrong.
presentation.hostLeadIn: Languages have logic. You seem to lack it.
wrongAnswerQuips: Java? James Gosling. No.
C++? Stroustrup. Learn names.
Ruby? Matsumoto. Wrong again.

questions.question: What was the name of the first general-purpose electronic digital computer, completed in 1945?
questions.explanation: ENIAC (Electronic Numerical Integrator and Computer) was designed by John Mauchly and J. Presper Eckert.

presentation.questionStyled: First **digital computer**? Room-sized behemoth. What's the name?
presentation.explanationStyled: ENIAC. It did math for artillery. You can't even do that.
presentation.hostLeadIn: Computers were once rooms, not pockets.
wrongAnswerQuips: UNIVAC was commercial. Wrong.
Colossus was codebreaking. Wrong.
Altair? That's a hobby kit. Fail.

questions.question: Which company acquired the mobile startup Android Inc. in 2005?
questions.explanation: Google acquired Android Inc. in July 2005 for at least $50 million.

presentation.questionStyled: Who bought **Android**? Look at your phone, if you can.
presentation.explanationStyled: Google. They bought it in 2005. It clearly paid off.
presentation.hostLeadIn: Acquisitions change the tech landscape.
wrongAnswerQuips: Microsoft failed at mobile. Sad.
Apple? Never. They keep it closed.
Facebook? They don't make hardware.

questions.question: In the context of the RSA encryption algorithm, what do the initials 'RSA' stand for?
questions.explanation: RSA is named after Ron Rivest, Adi Shamir, and Leonard Adleman, who first publicly described the algorithm in 1977.

presentation.questionStyled: What are the initials **RSA** in encryption? Don't make me spell it out.
presentation.explanationStyled: Rivest, Shamir, and Adleman. It’s an algorithm. Learn it.
presentation.hostLeadIn: Security relies on math. Math is hard for you.
wrongAnswerQuips: Random? No. Try again.
Remote? You're inventing acronyms.
Rapid? Just... no.

questions.question: The global climate cooling event known as the 'Year Without a Summer' in 1816 was primarily caused by the eruption of which volcano?
questions.explanation: The 1815 eruption of Mount Tambora in Indonesia released enough volcanic ash into the atmosphere to lower global temperatures for a year.

presentation.questionStyled: In 1816, humanity collectively forgot how to summer. Which volcano threw such a tantrum it cooled the planet?
presentation.explanationStyled: It was Mount Tambora. In 1815, it unleashed enough ash to ruin the weather for everyone, proving nature cares very little for our travel plans.
presentation.hostLeadIn: Let us take a brisk, chilly walk through historical climate disasters.
wrongAnswerQuips: Vesuvius was far too busy with Pompeii.
Krakatoa waited until 1883 to be truly impressive.
Pinatubo is a much more modern inconvenience.

questions.question: Which Babylonian king is best known for a set of 282 laws inscribed on a diorite stele?
questions.explanation: Hammurabi's Code is one of the oldest and best-preserved legal codes in human history.

presentation.questionStyled: Which Babylonian king decided that 282 laws deserved a permanent spot on a diorite slab?
presentation.explanationStyled: That would be Hammurabi. His code is a classic—literally one of the oldest sets of rules meant to keep civilization from eating itself.
presentation.hostLeadIn: Brush up on your ancient legalese, won't you?
wrongAnswerQuips: Nebuchadnezzar preferred building gardens to writing rules.
Sargon was busy building an empire, not paperwork.
Gilgamesh was too busy looking for immortality.

questions.question: The 1899-1901 uprising in China directed against foreign influence and Christian missionaries is known as what?
questions.explanation: The Boxer Rebellion was initiated by the 'Militia United in Righteousness' and opposed foreign imperialism in China.

presentation.questionStyled: The 1899-1901 uprising in China against foreign influence is known by what snappy revolutionary moniker?
presentation.explanationStyled: The Boxer Rebellion. The 'Militia United in Righteousness' certainly didn't think their nickname was as pugilistic as the world eventually made it sound.
presentation.hostLeadIn: Let us see if you can handle some turn-of-the-century geopolitical unrest.
wrongAnswerQuips: The Taiping Rebellion was a much earlier disappointment.
An Lushan was busy much, much earlier.
Red Turbans preferred a different century entirely.

questions.question: The European Recovery Program, designed to rebuild Western Europe after World War II, is commonly known as what?
questions.explanation: Named after Secretary of State George Marshall, the plan provided billions in economic aid to European nations.

presentation.questionStyled: Post-WWII Europe needed a serious makeover. What was the economic aid package commonly called?
presentation.explanationStyled: The Marshall Plan. George Marshall essentially bribed a continent into stability, and quite successfully, too.
presentation.hostLeadIn: Let us see if you understand the price of rebuilding empires.
wrongAnswerQuips: The Truman Doctrine was more about ideology.
The Molotov Plan was the Soviet version of 'no'.
The Atlantic Charter was an earlier diplomatic chat.

questions.question: Which group of elements on the periodic table is characterized by being chemically inert and having a full outer electron shell?
questions.explanation: Noble gases such as Helium and Neon are highly stable because they have complete valence shells.

presentation.questionStyled: Which periodic group is so socially awkward they refuse to interact with anyone else?
presentation.explanationStyled: The Noble Gases. With a full outer shell, they’re far too stable to care about forming chemical bonds with the likes of you.
presentation.hostLeadIn: Chemists can be so dramatic about their elements.
wrongAnswerQuips: Halogens are far too eager to react.
Alkali metals are dangerously clingy.
Alkaline earth metals are just trying too hard.

questions.question: According to the Heisenberg Uncertainty Principle, it is impossible to simultaneously know the exact position and which other property of a particle?
questions.explanation: Werner Heisenberg's principle states that the more precisely position is determined, the less precisely momentum can be known.

presentation.questionStyled: According to Heisenberg, if you know where a particle is, you cannot possibly know its what?
presentation.explanationStyled: Its momentum. It is the fundamental impossibility of pinning down a tiny object, a concept that likely mirrors your current confusion.
presentation.hostLeadIn: Quantum mechanics: where everything is uncertain, much like your performance.
wrongAnswerQuips: Mass is reasonably constant, really.
Charge doesn't just vanish like that.
Spin is a quantum property, but not the answer.

questions.question: The Haber-Bosch process is used industrially to synthesize which chemical compound used primarily in fertilizers?
questions.explanation: The Haber-Bosch process fixes atmospheric nitrogen with hydrogen to produce ammonia.

presentation.questionStyled: The Haber-Bosch process is why we can feed billions, but what chemical are they actually making?
presentation.explanationStyled: Ammonia. It’s the backbone of global fertilizer production, proving that chemistry is often just a fancy way to make things grow.
presentation.hostLeadIn: Let us see if you know your industrial chemical synthesis.
wrongAnswerQuips: Sulfuric acid is much too corrosive for breakfast.
Nitric oxide is for laughing, not farming.
Potash is a mineral, not a process output.

questions.question: The endosymbiotic theory suggests that which organelle originated as a free-living prokaryote?
questions.explanation: Mitochondria and chloroplasts are believed to have evolved from bacteria that were engulfed by ancestral eukaryotic cells.

presentation.questionStyled: Which cell organelle was once a free-living prokaryote before it decided to move in?
presentation.explanationStyled: The mitochondria. It traded its freedom for a permanent job generating ATP, which is a classic corporate compromise.
presentation.hostLeadIn: Cell biology, where every organelle has a tragic backstory.
wrongAnswerQuips: Ribosomes are just tiny protein factories.
The Golgi apparatus is the cell's post office.
Lysosomes are just the garbage collectors.

questions.question: What is the term for the temperature and pressure at which water can exist as a solid, liquid, and gas simultaneously?
questions.explanation: The triple point of water occurs at exactly 273.16 K (0.01 °C) and a partial vapor pressure of 611.657 pascals.

presentation.questionStyled: What is the thermodynamic sweet spot where water exists as solid, liquid, and gas simultaneously?
presentation.explanationStyled: The triple point. It’s the rare moment when water reaches equilibrium—a state you clearly have yet to achieve.
presentation.hostLeadIn: I hope you have the temperature and pressure correct for this one.
wrongAnswerQuips: The critical point is just a high-pressure phase.
The boiling point is far too hot for balance.
The dew point is just for condensation, darling.

questions.question: The bending of light as it passes from one medium to another of different optical density is known as what?
questions.explanation: Refraction is caused by the change in the speed of light when transitioning between materials with different refractive indices.

presentation.questionStyled: What do we call the bending of light as it passes between different mediums?
presentation.explanationStyled: Refraction. Light changes speed and bends, much like how your logic bends under the slightest bit of pressure.
presentation.hostLeadIn: Physics is full of bending truths, isn't it?
wrongAnswerQuips: Reflection is just light bouncing off mirrors.
Diffraction is light dancing around corners.
Dispersion is just a rainbow-making trick.

questions.question: Which 1927 silent film was the first ever winner of the Academy Award for Best Picture (then called Outstanding Picture)?
choices: Sunrise: A Song of Two Humans
questions.explanation: Wings, a World War I aviation film, remains the only silent film to win Best Picture until The Artist (2011).

presentation.questionStyled: Which silent film was the first to win Best Picture at the inaugural Oscars?
presentation.explanationStyled: Wings. An aviation epic that managed to win without saying a single word. Almost as impressive as your silence during this question.
presentation.hostLeadIn: A bit of cinematic trivia for the cultured few.
wrongAnswerQuips: The Jazz Singer was too busy with sound.
Metropolis was busy being too weird for Hollywood.
Sunrise was lovely but missed the top prize.

questions.question: Released in 1973, which Pink Floyd album holds the record for the most weeks spent on the Billboard 200 chart?
choices: The Dark Side of the Moon
questions.explanation: The Dark Side of the Moon spent a record-breaking 900+ weeks on the Billboard charts.

presentation.questionStyled: Which Pink Floyd masterpiece spent more time on the charts than you’ve spent reading?
presentation.explanationStyled: The Dark Side of the Moon. 900 weeks on the Billboard charts. Clearly, even the general public has occasionally good taste.
presentation.hostLeadIn: Let us see if you recognize a classic.
wrongAnswerQuips: The Wall is just a brick in your head.
Wish You Were Here is slightly less popular.
Animals is just a bit too barking.

questions.question: Which Studio Ghibli film became the first non-English language film to win the Academy Award for Best Animated Feature?
questions.explanation: Spirited Away, directed by Hayao Miyazaki, won the Oscar in 2003.

presentation.questionStyled: Which Studio Ghibli gem was the first non-English winner of the Best Animated Feature Oscar?
presentation.explanationStyled: Spirited Away. A surreal, breathtaking masterpiece that easily outperformed the western competition in 2003.
presentation.hostLeadIn: Animation that actually requires a brain to process.
wrongAnswerQuips: Totoro is precious, but not an Oscar winner.
Princess Mononoke was far too grim for them.
Howl's Moving Castle arrived a bit later.

questions.question: Unlike 'single-camera' shows, 'multi-camera' sitcoms are traditionally filmed in what setting?
choices: In front of a live studio audience
questions.explanation: Multi-camera sitcoms like Seinfeld or Friends are shot on a soundstage with a live audience.

presentation.questionStyled: Multi-camera sitcoms love the studio audience, but why are they filmed that way?
presentation.explanationStyled: It’s about capturing that live studio audience reaction. It adds a certain 'laugh track' quality, even if the jokes aren't actually funny.
presentation.hostLeadIn: Television production is a science, albeit a fairly simple one.
wrongAnswerQuips: Location shoots are for dramas, mostly.
Handheld cameras are for the shaky stuff.
3D is a gimmick nobody actually wants.

questions.question: Who was the original voice actor for Mickey Mouse when the character debuted in Steamboat Willie?
questions.explanation: Walt Disney himself provided the voice for Mickey Mouse from 1928 until 1947.

presentation.questionStyled: Who provided the original voice for that famous mouse in Steamboat Willie?
presentation.explanationStyled: Walt Disney himself. He provided the squeaks for years before realizing he had better things to do.
presentation.hostLeadIn: Before he was a corporation, he was just a guy in a booth.
wrongAnswerQuips: Mel Blanc was busy doing everyone else's voice.
Clarence Nash was the voice of the duck.
Pinto Colvig voiced a dog, eventually.

questions.question: Which term describes the use of strong contrasts between light and dark to achieve a sense of volume in three-dimensional objects?
questions.explanation: Chiaroscuro was famously employed by artists like Caravaggio and Rembrandt to create dramatic lighting.

presentation.questionStyled: What is the artistic technique of using light and dark contrast to create volume?
presentation.explanationStyled: Chiaroscuro. It’s what makes a painting look three-dimensional instead of like a flat, boring smudge.
presentation.hostLeadIn: Let us see if you can appreciate high art.
wrongAnswerQuips: Sfumato is for blurry edges, not volume.
Impasto is just using too much paint.
Fresco is just painting on wet plaster.

questions.question: The 1913 premiere of which Igor Stravinsky ballet famously caused a riot in the audience in Paris?
questions.explanation: The Rite of Spring's avant-garde music and choreography were so provocative they incited a public disturbance.

presentation.questionStyled: Which ballet premiere was so avant-garde that it incited a literal riot?
presentation.explanationStyled: The Rite of Spring. Parisians in 1913 apparently had very fragile sensibilities regarding dissonant strings and odd rhythm.
presentation.hostLeadIn: Art that makes people scream, now that is a legacy.
wrongAnswerQuips: The Firebird was far too tame.
Petrushka was a puppet show, basically.
Pulcinella was a much later experiment.

questions.question: Georges Seurat is known for developing which painting technique using small, distinct dots of color?
questions.explanation: Pointillism relies on the viewer's eye to blend dots of color into a full image.

presentation.questionStyled: Georges Seurat is famous for what technique made entirely of little dots?
presentation.explanationStyled: Pointillism. It relies on your eye to do all the work of blending color. Lazy on his part, truly.
presentation.hostLeadIn: A painting method that requires more patience than you likely possess.
wrongAnswerQuips: Fauvism is for people who hate natural colors.
Cubism is just breaking things into squares.
Expressionism is just feeling too much.

questions.question: The Bauhaus school, a major influence on modern design, was founded by Walter Gropius in which country?
questions.explanation: The Bauhaus operated in Germany from 1919 to 1933 before being closed under pressure from the Nazi regime.

presentation.questionStyled: Walter Gropius founded the Bauhaus school in which country?
presentation.explanationStyled: Germany. It became the epicenter of modern design before the political climate made art quite difficult.
presentation.hostLeadIn: Architecture is rarely just about building things.
wrongAnswerQuips: Austria was busy elsewhere.
Switzerland is too neutral for this.
The Netherlands preferred their own style.

questions.question: Which Mexican artist is best known for her numerous self-portraits and works inspired by nature and artifacts of Mexico?
questions.explanation: Frida Kahlo's work often explored themes of identity, the human body, and death.

presentation.questionStyled: Which Mexican artist turned her life of pain into legendary self-portraits?
presentation.explanationStyled: Frida Kahlo. Her work remains iconic, unlike your grasp of art history.
presentation.hostLeadIn: She painted her own reality, which is more than most can claim.
wrongAnswerQuips: Leonora Carrington was a different kind of dreamer.
Remedios Varo loved a different aesthetic.
Diego Rivera painted big walls, not himself.

questions.question: What is unique about John Cage's 1952 musical composition titled 4'33"?
choices: It is played entirely on a prepared piano
The performers are instructed not to play their instruments
It is written in a microtonal scale
questions.explanation: 4'33" instructs the performers not to play their instruments; the 'music' is the ambient sounds of the environment.

presentation.questionStyled: John Cage's 4'33" is an avant-garde classic. What is it that you actually hear?
presentation.explanationStyled: Exactly nothing. Or rather, the ambient noise of the room. It’s genius, assuming you like listening to silence.
presentation.hostLeadIn: Musical silence—the peak of highbrow pretension.
wrongAnswerQuips: A prepared piano is far too musical.
100 percussionists would be a noisy disaster.
Microtonal scales are far too complex.

questions.question: The 'Elgin Marbles' are a collection of Classical Greek marble sculptures that originally belonged to which building?
questions.explanation: Removed by Lord Elgin in the early 19th century, the marbles are currently housed in the British Museum.

presentation.questionStyled: The Elgin Marbles were originally stripped from which iconic building?
presentation.explanationStyled: The Parthenon. Now they sit in the British Museum, a permanent reminder of imperial souvenir-taking.
presentation.hostLeadIn: I love a good historical controversy over stolen rocks.
wrongAnswerQuips: The Temple of Zeus had its own problems.
The Erechtheion is just nearby, pay attention.
The Temple of Hephaestus remains intact.

questions.question: In 1863, which exhibition was established to display works rejected by the official Paris Salon?
questions.explanation: The Salon des Refusés featured works by Manet and others, paving the way for the Impressionist movement.

presentation.questionStyled: In 1863, rejected artists held an exhibition. What was this collection of outcasts called?
presentation.explanationStyled: The Salon des Refusés. It turned out that the people the official salon rejected were actually the ones defining the future of art.
presentation.hostLeadIn: The establishment never knows what it's missing, do they?
wrongAnswerQuips: The Salon d'Automne was too late to this.
The Salon des Indépendants was a later effort.
The Salon de la Rose + Croix was weird.

questions.question: Who is the only golfer to have won the 'Pre-Masters' Grand Slam, winning all four major championships in a single calendar year?
questions.explanation: In 1930, Bobby Jones won the U.S. Open, U.S. Amateur, British Open, and British Amateur.

presentation.questionStyled: Who holds the record for the only pre-Masters Grand Slam in golf history?
presentation.explanationStyled: Bobby Jones. He did it in 1930 and then retired, presumably because he had nothing left to prove to the rest of you.
presentation.hostLeadIn: Golf trivia for the man who insists on wearing plaid.
wrongAnswerQuips: Jack Nicklaus had a good run, but no.
Tiger Woods would have preferred it.
Arnold Palmer liked the tea more.

questions.question: Which player broke the Major League Baseball color barrier in 1947 when he debuted for the Brooklyn Dodgers?
questions.explanation: Jackie Robinson became the first African American to play in the MLB in the modern era.

presentation.questionStyled: Which baseball hero shattered the color barrier in 1947?
presentation.explanationStyled: Jackie Robinson. He played well enough to prove that talent shouldn't have been segregated in the first place.
presentation.hostLeadIn: A bit of American history, try not to strike out.
wrongAnswerQuips: Willie Mays followed soon after.
Hank Aaron had his own records to break.
Satchel Paige was a legend regardless.

questions.question: The word 'soccer' originated as a slang abbreviation for which term?
questions.explanation: The term 'soccer' comes from 'assoc.', an abbreviation of 'Association Football'.

presentation.questionStyled: The word 'soccer' is actually a slang abbreviation for which original term?
presentation.explanationStyled: Association Football. It’s an abbreviation of 'assoc.', proving that naming things is often a game of lazy shorthand.
presentation.hostLeadIn: A linguistic question for those who argue about the sport's name.
wrongAnswerQuips: Social football isn't even a thing.
Society football is far too formal.
Sectional football sounds like a map project.

questions.question: In the Tour de France, why is the leader's jersey (Maillot Jaune) colored yellow?
choices: It was the color of the first winner's bike
It matched the yellow paper of the sponsoring newspaper L'Auto
Yellow is the color of speed in French culture
It was the only fabric color available in 1919
questions.explanation: The jersey color was chosen to promote the newspaper L'Auto, which was printed on yellow paper.

presentation.questionStyled: The yellow jersey in the Tour de France is just a marketing gimmick for what?
presentation.explanationStyled: The newspaper L'Auto. They printed on yellow paper, so they chose a yellow jersey. Capitalism at its most vibrant.
presentation.hostLeadIn: Cycling history—it's mostly just advertising on wheels.
wrongAnswerQuips: The first winner's bike was not yellow.
Speed has no color, don't be ridiculous.
Fabric availability was rarely the issue.

questions.question: Which country hosted and won the first ever FIFA World Cup in 1930?
questions.explanation: Uruguay defeated Argentina 4-2 in the final of the inaugural World Cup held in Montevideo.

presentation.questionStyled: Which country won the inaugural FIFA World Cup in 1930?
presentation.explanationStyled: Uruguay. They hosted it, played well, and took the trophy. A dream scenario, really.
presentation.hostLeadIn: FIFA was a very different beast back then.
wrongAnswerQuips: Brazil would take much longer to shine.
Argentina lost the final, better luck next time.
Italy waited until 1934 to join the fun.

questions.question: Who is often cited as the world's first computer programmer for her work on Charles Babbage's Analytical Engine?
questions.explanation: Lovelace wrote the first algorithm intended to be processed by a machine in the mid-19th century.

presentation.questionStyled: Who is credited with writing the first computer program way back in the 19th century?
presentation.explanationStyled: Ada Lovelace. She saw the potential for machines to do more than just add numbers, which is impressive for the 1840s.
presentation.hostLeadIn: Computing started with a woman, not a basement nerd.
wrongAnswerQuips: Grace Hopper was a pioneer, but later.
Joan Clarke was a codebreaker, not this.
Margaret Hamilton sent us to the moon.

questions.question: In a URL, what does the 'S' in 'HTTPS' stand for?
questions.explanation: Hypertext Transfer Protocol Secure (HTTPS) uses encryption (SSL/TLS) to protect data in transit.

presentation.questionStyled: In 'HTTPS', what does the 'S' actually guarantee?
presentation.explanationStyled: It means the connection is Secure. Without it, you are just shouting your passwords into a crowded room.
presentation.hostLeadIn: Let's test your basic internet safety knowledge.
wrongAnswerQuips: Standard is too boring for this.
Service is too vague.
System is for IT people, not this.

questions.question: Moore's Law, an observation made by Gordon Moore in 1965, relates to the doubling of what every two years?
questions.explanation: Moore's Law predicted that the number of transistors on a microchip would double approximately every two years.

presentation.questionStyled: Moore's Law is about the doubling of what every two years?
presentation.explanationStyled: Transistors on a microchip. It’s the reason your phone isn't the size of a garage anymore.
presentation.hostLeadIn: Computers get smaller, unlike your ego.
wrongAnswerQuips: Internet speed is far too erratic.
Battery capacity is a struggle, obviously.
Hard drive storage grows, but that's not Moore.

questions.question: What is the primary technical difference between a Solid State Drive (SSD) and a Hard Disk Drive (HDD)?
choices: SSDs are only for external use
questions.explanation: Unlike HDDs, which use spinning disks, SSDs use NAND-based flash memory, making them faster and more durable.

presentation.questionStyled: What is the key structural difference between an SSD and an HDD?
presentation.explanationStyled: No moving parts. SSDs are flash-based, meaning they won't break when you drop them, which is perfect for clumsy people.
presentation.hostLeadIn: Hardware upgrades for the technically inept.
wrongAnswerQuips: SSDs are magnetic-free, dear.
HDDs have flash memory? In your dreams.
SSDs are for everyone, not just external use.

questions.question: Which company produced the 4004, the world's first commercially available microprocessor, in 1971?
questions.explanation: The Intel 4004 was a 4-bit central processing unit and the first complete CPU on a single chip.

presentation.questionStyled: Which company released the 4004, the first commercial microprocessor?
presentation.explanationStyled: Intel. It was a 4-bit chip that changed everything, assuming you enjoy being glued to screens.
presentation.hostLeadIn: Silicon Valley history: where it all went wrong.
wrongAnswerQuips: IBM was too busy with giant mainframes.
AMD was just getting started.
Motorola was doing their own thing.

questions.question: Which frequency range is commonly referred to as 'millimeter wave' (mmWave) in the context of 5G technology?
questions.explanation: Millimeter wave frequencies allow for higher data rates but have shorter range and less penetration power.

presentation.questionStyled: What frequency range is the so-called millimeter wave (mmWave) for 5G?
presentation.explanationStyled: 24 GHz to 100 GHz. It's fast, sure, but it can't even get through a window, let alone your thick skull.
presentation.hostLeadIn: 5G is the future, or so they tell us.
wrongAnswerQuips: That's just old radio range.
Mid-band is where the common stuff lives.
That's far too low-frequency for mmWave.

questions.question: Who is the creator of the Python programming language, first released in 1991?
questions.explanation: Guido van Rossum designed Python as a successor to the ABC language with a focus on code readability.

presentation.questionStyled: Who created the Python programming language in 1991?
presentation.explanationStyled: Guido van Rossum. He made it readable, which is the only reason you might have a chance at learning it.
presentation.hostLeadIn: Programming languages are all about the personality of the creator.
wrongAnswerQuips: Stroustrup made C++, the headache machine.
Gosling is the Java fellow.
Ritchie made C, for the brave.

questions.question: The 1325 'War of the Bucket' was a conflict fought between which two Italian city-states?
questions.explanation: The War of the Bucket took place between the city-states of Modena and Bologna, sparked by Modenese soldiers stealing a wooden bucket from a public well in Bologna.

presentation.questionStyled: In 1325, two Italian city-states went to war over a bucket. Which ones?
presentation.explanationStyled: It was indeed Modena and Bologna. Honestly, fighting a war over a wooden pail really puts human civilization’s maturity into perspective, doesn't it?
presentation.hostLeadIn: Prepare for a lesson in medieval pettiness.
wrongAnswerQuips: Too busy painting, I suppose.
They had ships to worry about.
War is rarely that logical.

questions.question: The 1618 incident known as the Second Defenestration of Prague served as the primary catalyst for which major conflict?
choices: The War of the Spanish Succession
questions.explanation: The throwing of Catholic regents out of a window by Protestant activists in Prague triggered the Thirty Years' War.

presentation.questionStyled: The 1618 Second Defenestration of Prague—basically, throwing people out of windows—triggered which massive conflict?
presentation.explanationStyled: It was the Thirty Years' War. Nothing kicks off a continental bloodbath quite like hurling religious bureaucrats out of a window.
presentation.hostLeadIn: Let’s discuss the most aggressive way to exit a building.
wrongAnswerQuips: A bit too early for that.
History is not your strong suit.
Wrong century, try harder.

questions.question: Which treaty formally ended the War of 1812 between the United States and the United Kingdom?
questions.explanation: The Treaty of Ghent, signed in December 1814 in modern-day Belgium, ended the War of 1812, restoring boundaries to 'status quo ante bellum'.

presentation.questionStyled: Which treaty officially brought the War of 1812 to a rather anti-climactic conclusion?
presentation.explanationStyled: The Treaty of Ghent. It essentially returned everything to how it was before everyone started shooting, which is a lovely way to waste lives and resources.
presentation.hostLeadIn: Let's see if you know how this pointless war ended.
wrongAnswerQuips: Wrong continent entirely.
That ended a much bigger war.
That was a different century.

questions.question: Among the naturally occurring noble gases, which one has the highest atomic mass?
questions.explanation: Radon (Rn) is the heaviest naturally occurring noble gas with an atomic number of 86. Oganesson is heavier but is synthetic.

presentation.questionStyled: Among the naturally occurring noble gases, which holds the heavyweight title for highest atomic mass?
presentation.explanationStyled: Radon is the answer. It’s heavy, radioactive, and generally best avoided. Good job if you got that, though I suspect you guessed.
presentation.hostLeadIn: Let's test your grasp of the periodic table.
wrongAnswerQuips: Not quite heavy enough.
A bit too light, perhaps?
Common, but not the heaviest.

questions.question: Which substance is used as the standard reference for determining the specific heat capacity scale, possessing a value of 4.184 J/g°C?
questions.explanation: Liquid water has a high specific heat capacity, often used as a benchmark in thermodynamics.

presentation.questionStyled: Which substance is the thermodynamic benchmark for specific heat capacity, sitting at 4.184 J/g°C?
presentation.explanationStyled: It's water. You're mostly made of it, so it’s rather fitting you should recognize it as the standard for thermal stability.
presentation.hostLeadIn: Back to the basics of physics for you.
wrongAnswerQuips: That is a metal, not a benchmark.
Too volatile for a standard.
Don't be cold, that's wrong.

questions.question: Which principle states that two identical fermions cannot occupy the same quantum state simultaneously?
questions.explanation: The Pauli Exclusion Principle, formulated by Wolfgang Pauli, is fundamental for understanding the structure of atoms and the behavior of electrons.

presentation.questionStyled: Which principle posits that two identical fermions cannot occupy the same quantum state at once?
presentation.explanationStyled: The Pauli Exclusion Principle. It’s essentially the 'no loitering' sign of the subatomic world, and thank heavens for it, or matter wouldn't exist.
presentation.hostLeadIn: Trying to wrap your head around quantum mechanics?
wrongAnswerQuips: That’s about uncertainty, not occupancy.
That’s the math, not the rule.
Not quite, but nice try.

questions.question: What is the quark composition of a meson?
questions.explanation: Mesons are hadronic subatomic particles composed of one quark and one antiquark, bound together by strong interactions.

presentation.questionStyled: What is the quark construction of a meson, if you please?
presentation.explanationStyled: A quark and an antiquark. It’s a messy little pair that annihilates itself eventually—much like your chances of winning if you didn't know this.
presentation.hostLeadIn: A tiny bit of particle physics for the masses.
wrongAnswerQuips: That’s a baryon, try again.
That’s not how it works.
Too many quarks, too little sense.

questions.question: Which 1991 movie was the first animated feature film to be nominated for the Academy Award for Best Picture?
questions.explanation: Disney's 'Beauty and the Beast' (1991) made history by becoming the first animated film to receive a nomination in the Best Picture category.

presentation.questionStyled: Which 1991 animated feature managed to charm the Academy into a Best Picture nomination?
presentation.explanationStyled: Disney's Beauty and the Beast. It was a rather impressive feat for a bunch of drawings to elbow their way into the Best Picture category.
presentation.hostLeadIn: Time to see if you remember 90s cinema history.
wrongAnswerQuips: A classic, but no.
That came a few years later.
Not quite, though very popular.

questions.question: In Cameron Crowe's 2000 film 'Almost Famous', what is the name of the fictional rock band that the protagonist follows?
questions.explanation: Stillwater is the fictional rock band in the film, which was loosely based on Crowe's experiences touring with bands like Led Zeppelin and The Allman Brothers.

presentation.questionStyled: In Almost Famous, what is the name of the fictional rock band our protagonist is sent to cover?
presentation.explanationStyled: Stillwater. It’s a quintessential 'fictional' band name, perfectly capturing that 70s rock ego.
presentation.hostLeadIn: Dusting off some classic rock cinema for the cultured.
wrongAnswerQuips: That was Tom Hanks' band.
That was definitely a different movie.
That band actually exists, tragically.

questions.question: The 1983 series finale of which television show remains the most-watched episode of a scripted series in U.S. television history?
questions.explanation: The finale of M*A*S*H, titled 'Goodbye, Farewell and Amen', was watched by over 105 million viewers.

presentation.questionStyled: Which 1983 series finale still holds the record for the most-watched scripted episode in U.S. history?
presentation.explanationStyled: M*A*S*H. Apparently, half the country had nothing better to do on a Tuesday than watch doctors leave a tent. Remarkable.
presentation.hostLeadIn: Television history isn't always as riveting as you'd hope.
wrongAnswerQuips: A close second, but no.
Not even close.
Popular, but not that popular.

questions.question: Which actor was the first to portray James Bond on screen, appearing in a 1954 television adaptation of 'Casino Royale'?
questions.explanation: Barry Nelson played James Bond in an episode of the anthology series 'Climax!' eight years before Sean Connery's 'Dr. No'.

presentation.questionStyled: Who was the first actor to portray James Bond, appearing in 1954? (Hint: It wasn't who you think.)
presentation.explanationStyled: It was Barry Nelson. I realize that doesn't fit the 'debonair spy' image, but facts rarely care about your cinematic preferences.
presentation.hostLeadIn: I bet you’re going to get this one wrong.
wrongAnswerQuips: Too obvious, darling.
He came much later.
A charming guess, but incorrect.

questions.question: Which 2001 film won the inaugural Academy Award for Best Animated Feature?
questions.explanation: DreamWorks' 'Shrek' won the first-ever Academy Award for Best Animated Feature, beating Pixar's 'Monsters, Inc.'

presentation.questionStyled: Which 2001 film secured the first-ever Academy Award for Best Animated Feature?
presentation.explanationStyled: Shrek. The industry clearly decided an ogre was worth an Oscar before a masterpiece. How delightful.
presentation.hostLeadIn: Let’s look at the history of awards and cartoons.
wrongAnswerQuips: Too scary for the voters.
That's a bit too young.
A masterpiece, but not the first.

questions.question: Which 17th-century Dutch artist painted the massive group portrait officially titled 'Militia Company of District II under the Command of Captain Frans Banninck Cocq'?
questions.explanation: Better known as 'The Night Watch', this masterpiece was painted by Rembrandt in 1642.

presentation.questionStyled: Which Dutch master painted the iconic group portrait known to everyone as 'The Night Watch'?
presentation.explanationStyled: Rembrandt van Rijn. If you didn't know that, do try to visit a museum once in your life.
presentation.hostLeadIn: I trust your art history knowledge isn't entirely blank.
wrongAnswerQuips: Wrong era entirely.
Wrong artist, wrong vibes.
He preferred domestic scenes.

questions.question: Which artistic movement was founded by the publication of Filippo Tommaso Marinetti's manifesto in 1909, emphasizing speed and technology?
questions.explanation: Futurism originated in Italy and glorified modernity, industry, and mechanical progress.

presentation.questionStyled: Which artistic movement was spawned by a 1909 manifesto, obsessed with speed, technology, and violence?
presentation.explanationStyled: Futurism. A charmingly loud movement that thought machines were the future. They were half-right.
presentation.hostLeadIn: A question for those who pretend to read manifestos.
wrongAnswerQuips: Too nihilistic for this.
Too dreamlike for this.
Too logical for this.

questions.question: What type of stone was primarily used to carve the ancient Greek statue 'Winged Victory of Samothrace'?
questions.explanation: The statue is made of white Parian marble, while the ship's prow base is made of grey Lartos marble.

presentation.questionStyled: What kind of stone forms the Winged Victory of Samothrace?
presentation.explanationStyled: Parian marble. It’s quite lovely, really. I’m surprised you didn’t just guess 'stone' and hope for the best.
presentation.hostLeadIn: Let's check your knowledge of ancient materials.
wrongAnswerQuips: Too soft for a sculpture.
Wrong Mediterranean island.
Not exactly the right texture.

questions.question: Which English composer is known for the 'Enigma Variations', where each variation is a musical sketch of one of his close friends?
questions.explanation: Edward Elgar's 'Variations on an Original Theme', Op. 36, is popularly known as the Enigma Variations.

presentation.questionStyled: Which English composer wrote the Enigma Variations, a musical tribute to his friends?
presentation.explanationStyled: Edward Elgar. It’s quite the endearing gesture, provided his friends actually appreciated his music, of course.
presentation.hostLeadIn: If you enjoy classical music, this should be simple.
wrongAnswerQuips: Too modern for that sound.
He was busy with planets.
A different type of English composer.

questions.question: Who designed the titanium-clad Guggenheim Museum in Bilbao, Spain, which opened in 1997?
questions.explanation: The Guggenheim Bilbao is one of Frank Gehry's most famous works and is considered a landmark of contemporary architecture.

presentation.questionStyled: Which architect is responsible for the titanium-clad, sculptural marvel that is the Guggenheim Bilbao?
presentation.explanationStyled: Frank Gehry. He essentially took a crumpled piece of paper and called it art. And people loved it.
presentation.hostLeadIn: Architecture: the art we're forced to look at.
wrongAnswerQuips: He's been dead a while.
She does houses, not museums.
Wrong century for his style.

questions.question: Which technology company originally developed the Java programming language in the mid-1990s?
questions.explanation: Java was developed by James Gosling and his team at Sun Microsystems, which was later acquired by Oracle.

presentation.questionStyled: Which company originally birthed the Java programming language back in the 90s?
presentation.explanationStyled: Sun Microsystems. Ah, the company that gave us Java before Oracle gobbled them up. A classic tech story.
presentation.hostLeadIn: A little bit of tech nostalgia for the nerds.
wrongAnswerQuips: They wanted to destroy it.
They have their own languages.
They prefer devices, not code.

questions.question: What is the standard TCP port number used for secure HTTP (HTTPS) connections?
questions.explanation: Port 443 is the standard port for HTTPS traffic, whereas port 80 is used for unencrypted HTTP.

presentation.questionStyled: What is the standard TCP port number for secure HTTPS connections?
presentation.explanationStyled: It’s 443. If you don't know this, I genuinely worry about what you're doing on the internet.
presentation.hostLeadIn: I expect you to know this simple networking fact.
wrongAnswerQuips: That's for the old stuff.
That's for unencrypted traffic.
That's an alternative, not standard.

questions.question: Which noble gas, with atomic number 18, makes up approximately 0.93% of the Earth's atmosphere?
questions.explanation: Argon is the third most abundant gas in the atmosphere, making it significantly more common than carbon dioxide.

presentation.questionStyled: Which noble gas constitutes 0.93% of the atmosphere?
presentation.explanationStyled: Argon. I hope you aren't breathing it in, but you should know it.
presentation.hostLeadIn: Chemistry time. Try not to fail this one.
wrongAnswerQuips: Neon? You're guessing based on signs.
Krypton is a trace element. Wrong.
Xenon is too rare. Fail.

questions.question: What does the 'R' stand for in the gene-editing technology known as CRISPR?
questions.explanation: CRISPR stands for Clustered Regularly Interspaced Short Palindromic Repeats.

presentation.questionStyled: What does the 'R' in CRISPR actually mean?
presentation.explanationStyled: Repeats. As in Clustered Regularly Interspaced Short Palindromic Repeats. Simple.
presentation.hostLeadIn: Analyzing the code of life; try to keep up.
wrongAnswerQuips: Random? You’re clearly guessing.
DNA replicates, but that's not the name.
Ribosomal? Wrong branch of science entirely.

questions.question: During an action potential, which ion primarily enters the neuron to cause depolarization?
questions.explanation: The rapid influx of sodium ions (Na+) into the cell causes the membrane potential to become more positive, leading to depolarization.

presentation.questionStyled: Which ion enters a neuron to cause depolarization?
presentation.explanationStyled: Sodium. Na+ flood the gate. Pay attention.
presentation.hostLeadIn: A look at the brain—yours is apparently firing blanks.
wrongAnswerQuips: Potassium leaves, it doesn't cause the spike.
Chloride is inhibitory. You're guessing.
Calcium is for signaling, not the voltage.

questions.question: What was the original name of the YouTube sensation 'Nyan Cat' when it was first uploaded as a GIF?
questions.explanation: The creator, Chris Torres, originally titled the animated GIF 'Pop-Tart Cat' before it was paired with the vocaloid song on YouTube.

presentation.questionStyled: What was the original name for the Nyan Cat GIF?
presentation.explanationStyled: Pop-Tart Cat. Honestly, internet history is the only thing you might know.
presentation.hostLeadIn: Diving into the digital archives. Don't embarrass me.
wrongAnswerQuips: Too literal. Try again.
Space Cat? You wish.
Neon Kitty? You’re just making stuff up.

questions.question: What was the title of Billie Eilish's debut studio album released in 2019?
choices: When We All Fall Asleep, Where Do We Go?
questions.explanation: Billie Eilish's 2019 debut album swept the four main categories at the 62nd Grammy Awards.

presentation.questionStyled: What was Billie Eilish's 2019 debut album?
presentation.explanationStyled: When We All Fall Asleep, Where Do We Go? It was everywhere. You had to have heard it.
presentation.hostLeadIn: Regarding modern music, try to prove you aren't living under a rock.

questions.question: Which actress and comedian was the first African American to achieve EGOT status (winning an Emmy, Grammy, Oscar, and Tony) by winning them all in a competitive capacity?
questions.explanation: Whoopi Goldberg is among the rare group of EGOT winners, having won her fourth award (an Emmy) in 2002.

presentation.questionStyled: Who was the first African American to attain EGOT status?
presentation.explanationStyled: Whoopi Goldberg. It’s hard to ignore, unlike your lack of trivia knowledge.
presentation.hostLeadIn: Awards. The only things that matter to these people.

questions.question: In what year did the high-profile celebrity couple Angelina Jolie and Brad Pitt officially announce their separation?
questions.explanation: After two years of marriage and over a decade together, Angelina Jolie filed for divorce from Brad Pitt in September 2016.

presentation.questionStyled: When did Brangelina finally call it quits?
presentation.explanationStyled: 2016. I’m surprised you remember this tabloid drama.

wrongAnswerQuips: That's when they got married. Wrong.

questions.question: In which minor key is the 'Winter' concerto from Antonio Vivaldi's 'The Four Seasons' composed?
questions.explanation: 'Winter' (L'inverno) is famously composed in F minor, creating a stark, chilly atmosphere.

presentation.questionStyled: In what key is Vivaldi’s Winter?
presentation.explanationStyled: F minor. Cold and dark, like my patience for this quiz.
presentation.hostLeadIn: A seasonal challenge. Try not to be tone-deaf.
wrongAnswerQuips: Summer is G minor. Pay attention.

questions.question: Who painted 'Liberty Leading the People,' commemorating the July Revolution of 1830 in France?
questions.explanation: The painting depicts a woman personifying Liberty as she leads the people over the barricades.

presentation.questionStyled: Who painted Liberty Leading the People?
presentation.explanationStyled: Eugène Delacroix. Romanticism at its peak. You’re welcome.
presentation.hostLeadIn: A revolutionary image. Try to keep up with the history.

questions.question: The FIFA World Cup has been held every four years since its inception in 1930, except for which two years due to World War II?
questions.explanation: The tournaments scheduled for 1942 and 1946 were cancelled, with play resuming in 1950.

presentation.questionStyled: Which two World Cup years were lost to WWII?
presentation.explanationStyled: 1942 and 1946. War usually interrupts sports, obviously.
presentation.hostLeadIn: The beautiful game... though you're failing the beautiful questions.
wrongAnswerQuips: 1938 happened. Check your calendar.

questions.question: On which specific surface is the Australian Open tennis tournament currently played?
questions.explanation: The Australian Open has been played on hard courts since 1988.

presentation.questionStyled: What surface is the Australian Open played on?
presentation.explanationStyled: Hard court. Are we done here?

questions.question: Who was the first ever Formula 1 World Drivers' Champion, winning the title in 1950?
questions.explanation: Giuseppe 'Nino' Farina won the inaugural championship driving for Alfa Romeo.

presentation.questionStyled: Who was the first F1 champion?
presentation.explanationStyled: Giuseppe Farina. Try to remember this one.
presentation.hostLeadIn: At high speed... try to keep up.
wrongAnswerQuips: Fangio won later. Keep reading.

questions.question: Which Dutch programmer created the Python programming language, first released in 1991?
questions.explanation: Guido van Rossum designed Python to be a highly readable language, named after Monty Python's Flying Circus.

presentation.questionStyled: Who created Python?
presentation.explanationStyled: Guido van Rossum. Don't look it up, just know it.

wrongAnswerQuips: Stroustrup? C++. Close, but no.

questions.question: What does the 'S' stand for in the RSA encryption algorithm?
questions.explanation: RSA stands for Rivest, Shamir, and Adleman, the surnames of the three researchers who first publicly described the algorithm.

presentation.questionStyled: What is the 'S' in RSA?
presentation.explanationStyled: Shamir. It’s an acronym of surnames. Learn it.
presentation.hostLeadIn: Encoded secrets; try to decode this without a meltdown.
